cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buyers' guide telling marketing leaders exactly what to probe before signing an AI contract — covering business value proof, data policies, and implementation realities. This matters because most AI purchasing decisions are still made on hype rather than evidence, and bad procurement locks teams into expensive, underperforming tools for years.

stories.head: Omneky Launches Public API and MCP Server for Autonomous Ad Creative Generation
stories.plain: Omneky opens its autonomous creative-generation engine to external developers and AI agents via a public API and Model Context Protocol server. This shifts ad creative from a closed SaaS tool to an embeddable capability any platform or agent can call, significantly lowering the barrier to agentic, always-on creative production at scale.

stories.head: AI Search Reshapes Brand Discovery: iSOFT and Somantra Partner on Answer Engine Optimisation
stories.plain: A new enterprise partnership targets Answer Engine Optimisation (AEO) — making brands visible inside AI-generated search results rather than traditional blue-link rankings. Aimed at banking, healthcare, and government sectors, this is an early signal that GEO is moving from experiment to budgeted enterprise service across regulated industries.

stories.head: Fastmail Launches EU-Hosted Email Infrastructure for Simpler GDPR Compliance
stories.plain: Fastmail is opening an Amsterdam data centre so customers can keep email data inside the EU, directly addressing data-residency and GDPR compliance concerns. As AI tools process more customer communications, knowing exactly where that data lives is becoming a procurement requirement, not just a legal nicety.

stories.head: Transactional, Triggered, and Promotional Emails: What's the Difference?
stories.plain: A clear explainer separating three email types that carry different legal obligations, revenue roles, and automation rules. With AI now auto-generating and auto-sending messages at scale, misclassifying email type can violate CAN-SPAM or GDPR — making this foundational knowledge critical for anyone governing an AI-powered messaging stack.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buyer's guide for marketing teams evaluating AI tools, covering business value, data policies, customer proof, and implementation readiness. This matters because most martech buyers lack a structured framework for cutting through vendor hype, and bad purchasing decisions are expensive to unwind once contracts are signed.

stories.head: AI adoption hits 90% as CX deployment paths diverge
stories.plain: New research shows AI is nearly universal in customer experience, but organizations are splitting sharply on architecture choices, governance models, and how to build customer trust while proving ROI. The divergence signals that the buying and building phase is over; execution and accountability are now the differentiators.

stories.head: Omneky launches public API and MCP Server, enabling autonomous ad creative generation for any platform or AI agent
stories.plain: Omneky opened its creative engine to developers and AI agents via a public API and Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, letting any platform or agent generate ad creative autonomously. This is a structural shift in how ad creative is built and integrated — it moves production from human workflows into agent-to-agent pipelines.

stories.head: The CMS is becoming the AI operating system for brands
stories.plain: Choosing a content management system now means evaluating it as the foundation for AI discovery, agentic workflows, personalization, and governance — not just content publishing. Brands that pick the wrong CMS today will find their AI ambitions structurally blocked, making this a high-stakes procurement decision.

stories.head: Context — not the model — now decides what your AI stack costs and whether it works
stories.plain: Analysis argues that the AI model itself is now a commodity; what separates ROI-positive deployments from money pits is the quality of data context, real-time signals, and machine-readable brand content fed to agents. Directly reshapes how marketers should budget and architect their AI stacks.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Agentic AI Takes the Wheel in Marketing Orchestration
body: The conversation has moved decisively past prompts and chatbots: autonomous AI agents are now being wired into marketing stacks to plan, execute, and iterate campaigns with minimal human hand-holding. Cresta's training simulator, Omneky's open API and MCP server, and Pega's orchestration framework all point to the same shift — agents are graduating from demos to production.

The practical implication for decision-makers is that workflow design, not prompt craft, is the new competitive skill. As one analyst framed it, the secret to scaling AI-assisted work isn't better prompts but better system architecture — meaning teams need to rethink how tasks are sequenced, handed off, and quality-checked across agent pipelines.

themes.head: AI Search Is Rewriting Brand Visibility Rules
body: AI-powered search engines are replacing the classic ten-blue-links model with synthesised answers — and brands that don't appear in those answers effectively don't exist to the searcher. The trust signals that once boosted PageRank (backlinks, domain authority) are giving way to signals of credibility and factual accuracy that LLMs reward.

Enterprises are already responding: iSOFT and Somantra have launched a joint practice specifically to help companies own their presence in AI-generated results. This marks a new category of search optimisation that sits somewhere between traditional SEO and reputation management, and it will require new measurement frameworks entirely.
ev: MarTech Series · AI search reshapes brand discovery

themes.head: The CMS & Stack Evolves Into an AI Operating System
body: Content management platforms are being repositioned as the central nervous system through which AI agents access, personalise, and publish brand content at scale. This is a meaningful architectural shift: the CMS is no longer a publishing tool but a runtime environment where context, permissions, and brand governance are enforced for every AI-generated output.

The adjacent insight from the Martech Futurist column reinforces this — context is now the primary cost driver and performance variable in an AI-enabled stack. Vendors who control the context layer (structured data, brand rules, customer history) will have outsized leverage over the rest of the stack regardless of which LLM sits on top.
ev: Agile Brand · Context decides stack cost

themes.head: AI Vendor Scrutiny Becomes a Core Buying Discipline
body: With AI adoption reportedly hitting 90% in customer-experience deployments, the market is maturing from experimentation to procurement — and buyers are realising they lack the frameworks to evaluate claims rigorously. MarTech's five-question vendor checklist and the diverging CX deployment paths story both signal that how you buy AI is becoming as strategic as what you buy.

Enterprise partnerships like LTM–Anthropic also show that delivery capability, not just model quality, is a differentiator. Organisations are shopping for implementation track records and data-governance commitments alongside benchmark scores, pushing vendors to compete on accountability rather than raw performance alone.
ev: MarTech · AI adoption & CX paths diverge

themes.head: AI Automates Execution, but Strategic Judgement Gaps Widen
body: As AI absorbs more of the tactical workload — writing copy, analysing funnels, running A/B tests — a structural question is surfacing: who develops the senior judgement that used to be built by doing the tactical work? The training pipeline for the next generation of marketers is broken if junior roles are automated before the learning they provide is replaced.

The companion pieces on funnel blind spots and misaligned GTM scaling underscore the same gap from a different angle: AI scales execution faster than strategy can be validated, which means teams can now be wrong at enormous speed and cost. The real competitive advantage is shifting to the humans who can identify which problems are worth solving before handing them to the machine.
ev: Agile Brand · Marketing funnel is lying
MarTech · Scaling the wrong part of GTM

month.takeaway: Across every layer of the stack, the month's signal is the same: AI is shifting power to whoever controls context, trust, and strategic judgement — not to whoever deploys the most automation.
